title: Academy Project Funding for Professor Outi Mäkitie for research on early-onset osteoporosis
content:
The project supported by the Research Council of Finland aims to identify new disease-causing genes in families with early-onset osteoporosis. We use novel genetic methods to identify the underlying gene mutations in families with early-onset osteoporosis. When the gene defect is detected, we use cell-based studies and zebrafish and mouse models to study the mechanisms through which the gene mutation leads to osteoporosis. In addition, we perform extensive musculoskeletal evaluation, including bone and muscle biopsies, to establish the clinical and tissue-level consequences of the disease-causing gene variants. This study provides new information about inherited osteoporosis and helps us to improve diagnosis and treatment of osteoporosis and related bone disorders.

title: In Memory of Victoria Sinclair
content:
The University of Helsinki and the Institute for Atmospheric and Earth System Research (INAR) mourn the loss of our colleague and friend, Senior University Lecturer Victoria Sinclair, who passed away in a tragic accident on 30 May 2026.

Victoria was an outstanding meteorologist, educator, and researcher whose contributions left a lasting impact on our community. She joined the University of Helsinki in 2009 and served as University Lecturer in Meteorology from 2019 onwards. In January 2026, she was appointed Senior University Lecturer, reflecting her excellence in teaching, research, and academic leadership.

Victoria was internationally recognized for her research in meteorology, particularly in atmospheric dynamics, extreme weather, and storms. She led several major research projects, supervised numerous doctoral and master's students, and served as leader of the Dynamic Meteorology Group. Her scientific work resulted in more than 60 peer-reviewed publications and contributed significantly to advances in weather and climate research. Many further scientific papers will be published in the coming years based on her work and the projects she initiated.

title: New transatlantic research network ATHENA advances mechanistic insights and translational applications in cardiovascular disease
content:
Atherosclerosis is the underlying cause of most heart attacks and strokes and remains one of the leading causes of death and disability worldwide. The five-year  (Atherosclerosis Targets from Human gEnetics and functional geNomic Approaches) consortium aims to identify the genetic and molecular pathways driving atherosclerosis by combining large-scale human genetics, functional genomics, and experimental validation in cellular and animal models.

Within ATHENA, FIMM will play a leading role in the consortium’s human genetics-driven discovery programme. Led by Professor Samuli Ripatti, the team will expand large-scale genetic analyses of cardiovascular disease in FinnGen and integrate them with proteomic and clinical data to identify causal disease genes and biological mechanisms underlying coronary artery disease and cerebrovascular disease.

title: Europe’s best AI literacy initiative for education comes from Finland
content:
There were 90 entries in the Digital Skills for Education category and 220 entries in the competition overall.

Generation AI is a multidisciplinary, multi-university research programme funded by the Strategic Research Council (SRC) within the Research Council of Finland. It develops research-based educational technologies, learning materials and learning packages to strengthen AI literacy. The project involves researchers from the University of Eastern Finland, the University of Helsinki, the University of Oulu and Code School Finland.

The programme helps children and young people understand the operating principles of AI and critically assess the ethical and societal impacts of AI-based technologies.

The educational technologies developed by the Generation AI research community enable pupils to explore the operating principles of AI without programming skills. For example, pupils can build simple classifiers or their own language model, examine how social media algorithms work and investigate the effects of algorithmic bias.
snippet: There were 90 entries in the Digital Skills for Education category and 220 entries in the competition overall.
creator: pimikkol@helsinki.fi

title: Tutors help new students settle into their new environment
content:
Hazhaow: My name is Hazhaow Murad, and I am a second-year Master's student in Food Sciences. When I started my studies, I sometimes found myself searching for information and answers on my own, which taught me how important timely guidance and support can be for new students. This inspired me to become a tutor so that I can help new students feel welcomed, informed, and confident from the beginning of their studies.

Olajumoke: My name is Olajumoke Sanni, and I am a second-year Master’s student in Animal Nutrition at the University of Helsinki. As a late-arriving student, I received valuable support from student tutors who helped me settle into university life and stay on track academically. Their guidance inspired me to become a tutor so that I can help new students feel welcomed, supported, and confident as they begin their studies.

title: Geenitieto auttaa ennustamaan vakavan silmäsairauden alkamista ja etenemistä
content:
Glaucoma is the leading cause of irreversible visual impairment worldwide. Because the disease often progresses without symptoms for years, many patients are diagnosed only after permanent damage to the optic nerve has already occurred. Earlier detection through targeted screening could help prevent vision loss.

A new study based on the FinnGen dataset, which includes hundreds of thousands of Finnish participants and more than 21,000 individuals with glaucoma, demonstrates that genetic information can be used to identify people at particularly high risk of developing the disease.

The researchers calculated a polygenic risk score for each participant by combining the effects of numerous genetic variants associated with glaucoma. Based on these scores, the population could be divided into groups with markedly different lifetime risks of developing the disease.

Among individuals whose polygenic risk score ranked in the highest one percent of the population, nearly half developed glaucoma during their lifetime. In contrast, fewer than three out of every one hundred individuals in the lowest one percent risk group developed the disease.

title: What does a medieval calf look like in mass spectrometry?
content:
At first glance, the peaks of a spectrum may not look much like a medieval calf. But in biocodicology, this is exactly where the animal begins to appear.

In the CHARM project, we use proteomic analysis ZooMS (Zooarchaeology by Mass Spectrometry), to identify the animal species used to make medieval parchment. Since parchment is produced from animal skin, it’s packed with collagen that is preserved even after hundreds of years.

The process begins with the careful collection of microscopic collagen fibers from the parchment surface. These proteins are then analysed with mass spectrometry, producing a spectrum filled with peaks. Each peak corresponds to fragments of collagen molecules with slightly different masses.

By selecting specific peaks and comparing them against published reference datasets, we can identify characteristic “markers” for different animals. Certain peak combinations indicate calf, others sheep or goat. A medieval calf, in this sense, becomes visible not through an image, but through a molecular fingerprint.
snippet: At first glance, the peaks of a spectrum may not look much like a medieval calf. But in biocodicology, this is exactly where the animal begins to appear.
creator: tuulikas@helsinki.fi

title: HEP-TED achievements take centre stage at International Conference on Re-shaping Education for Sustainable Development
content:
Organized under the theme “Building a Sustainable Future through Pedagogical Innovation, Inclusion and Resilience,” the conference brought together academics, researchers, policymakers, teacher educators, and development partners from Rwanda and beyond to explore how education can respond to the demands of a rapidly changing world. Among the conference highlights was the HEP-TED Symposium 1, convened under the theme “Advancing Pedagogy and Quality in Higher Education.”

The symposium provided a platform for reflection, dialogue, and knowledge sharing on contemporary challenges and opportunities in higher education, with a particular focus on pedagogical innovation, leadership, quality assurance, and institutional collaboration. More importantly, it showcased the impact of the Rwanda–Finland partnership in transforming teacher education and strengthening higher education systems.

title: Anca Tureanu challenges the basic assumptions of particle physics
content:
Neutrinos are extremely tiny elementary particles that are all around us. There are vast numbers of them — trillions pass through your body at this very moment — but they interact so weakly with other particles that they can only be detected with specialised instruments.

Despite their harmlessness, neutrinos pose a problem for our understanding of the universe, says , the new professor of particle physics at the University of Helsinki.

At the heart of the problem is a phenomenon called oscillation, in which a neutrino changes its type for no apparent reason.

There are three types, or so-called flavours, of neutrino: the electron neutrino, the muon neutrino and the tau neutrino, named according to which elementary particle reaction produces them.

Observations suggest, however, that neutrinos can change their flavour. According to Tureanu, this is a theoretical challenge.

– No other elementary particle changes its type while propagating freely, without interaction, she points out.

As a result, physicists developed a separate theory for neutrino conversions — one that essentially sits on top of the rest of particle physics.

Tureanu does not buy this theory.
snippet: Neutrinos are extremely tiny elementary particles that are all around us. There are vast numbers of them — trillions pass through your body at this very moment — but they interact so weakly with other
creator: merimaaj@helsinki.fi

title: Kesän puutarhaopastukset Kaisaniemessä
content:
On a guided tour, you’ll journey through millions of years of evolution along the paths of the Evolution Garden, and immerse yourself in a world of scents and colours in the Garden of the senses. You’ll also admire unique lichen and moss gardens, while hearing stories about the garden’s past and present, and the buildings found in the area.

Tours will take place from June 23 to August 22, 2026. Free entrance, no prior registration required.

  • Tuesdays, Fridays and Saturdays at 15 (3 pm)

    • The tour starts at the greenhouse entrance and lasts approximately 40 minutes.

    Kaisaniemenranta 2, Helsinki

title: Arkadiankadun siltaremontti aiheuttaa muutoksia liikennejärjestelyihin Luonnontieteellisen museon lähistöllä
content:
The renovation will start with the construction of temporary traffic arrangements from Wednesday 7 January onwards. The renovation is expected to be completed in late 2026.

A temporary bridge for pedestrians will be built on the east side of Arkadiankatu over the Baana. The aim is to maintain the pedestrian route on the east side of the bridge until the temporary bridge is operational. Cyclists will also be able to use the bridge.

The Baana route underneath the bridge is expected to remain open for most of the renovation's duration, with some minor exceptions.

There will be changes to the motor vehicle routes by the bridge and around it in January. If possible, should be favoured during the bridge renovation.

Connections to the bus terminals in Kamppi will remain available, but there will be a slight diversion of routes.

Trams 1 and 1T will run via Simonkatu and Kamppi. Lasipalatsi and the Natural History Museum will not be served.

The diversion is due to the renovation of the Arkadiankatu bridge and will remain in place until late 2026. The renovation will not affect HSL's bus services. 

Pohjoinen Rautatiekatu 13

title: Ukrainan sota - yliopiston suositukset
content:
The University of Helsinki has condemned the military action by Russia and expressed its support for Ukraine and its university community. We have also expressed our support for Ukraine, together with other Finnish universities, through statements by and the . As a demonstration of support, we have illuminated buildings in the colours of the Ukrainian flag and raised the flag on our premises.

One of the principles of the University of Helsinki is to promote international collaboration in research and higher education, and to support these efforts even in difficult circumstances. Russia’s indefensible military aggression in Ukraine has required exceptional measures, which include those taken in the collaboration between universities.

The University of Helsinki is monitoring both national and EU-level sanctions, adapting our policies and processes accordingly. Together with other Finnish and international universities, the University of Helsinki strives to promote the end of military action in Ukraine, while supporting Ukrainian institutions and people.
